macro_segments.title: Dangling Limbs and Protruding Fetal Limbs: Prohibition Status and Tumah Classification

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Prohibition of Dangling Limbs from a Slaughtered Animal
Mitzvas Poresh Bilvad: Rabbinic vs. Biblical Status of Dangling Limbs
Rambam's Alternative Understanding: Biblical Prohibition Without Lashes
Consequences of Eating Dangling Limbs: Malkos and Tumah Status
Dangling Limbs Do Not Convey Tumah: Status as Neither Neveilah nor Trefah

macro_segments.title: Dispute Between Rav Yosef and the Rabbanan: Dangling Limbs in Cases of Natural Death vs. Shechitah

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Initial Statement: Eating Dangling Limbs Incurs Lashes
Anonymous Objection and Alternative Tradition: No Lashes for Dangling Limbs
Rav Huna's Weighing of Traditions and Rav Yosef's Offense
Rav Yosef's Clarification: Malkos Only When Animal Dies Naturally (Achronut)
The Two Traditions Reconciled: Different Scenarios for Dangling Limbs

macro_segments.title: Derivation of the Principle That Death Creates Nifol (Falling) While Shechitah Does Not

micro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Source for the Distinction Between Misah and Shechitah
Derivation from Verse on Dead Shratzim: Misah Osei Nifol, Shechitah Eino Osei
Rav Ada's Challenge: How Can Shratzim Teach About Shechitah?
Resolution: Apply Teaching from Shratzim to Animals Where Shechitah Is Relevant
The Principle of Ke-ein Mitah: Moisture Requirement for Tumah
Double Usage of Bemotam in the Verse to Derive Both Principles

macro_segments.title: Dispute Over Dangling Limbs of Dead vs. Live Fetuses When Mother Is Slaughtered

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda: Different Rules for Live Fetus Limbs vs. Dead Fetus Limbs
Live Fetus Limb: Debate Between Rishonim on Status (Neveilah vs. Trefah)
Dead Fetus Limb: Shechitah Creates Nifol (All Agree)
Rava's Disagreement: Shechitah Never Creates Nifol, Even for Dead Fetus

macro_segments.title: Eight-Month-Old Baby: Non-Viability and the Status of Shechitah

micro_segments.title: Eight-Month-Old Baby Is Not Viable: Shechitah Does Not Help, Remains Neveilah
Baraita Challenge: If Shechitah Relevant by Species, Eight-Month Should Not Be Tamei
Resolution: Ein Bemino Shechitah (No Species-Applicable Shechitah for Eight-Month)
Shechitah of Mother Makes Eight-Month Baby Relevant: Ein Bemino Defeats the Distinction
Teaching from Verse: Trefah Whose Shechitah Works Does Not Cause Tumah

macro_segments.title: Question: Can a Fetus Be Slaughtered In Utero and What Is Its Status When Born

micro_segments.title: Rav Hoshaya's Question: Shechitah of Fetus While Still in Womb
Applicability to Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbanan: Debate Over When Shechitah Becomes Meaningful
Rava's Resolution via Baraita: Animal Born Treifah from Conception (Five Legs)

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Eight-Month vs. Nine-Month Baby; Alive vs. Dead; Debate on Shechitah of Mother

micro_segments.title: Case 1: Eight-Month Baby or Dead Nine-Month Baby—Shechitah of Mother Works
Case 2: Live Nine-Month Baby—Rabbi Meir: Requires Own Shechitah
Case 3: Live Nine-Month Baby—Rabbanan: Shechitah of Mother Suffices
Rabbi Shimon Shezuri: Shechitah of Mother Works Even for Grown Animal
Case Where Mother Not Slaughtered: Baby Requires Own Shechitah (All Agree)

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Chelev and Gid Hanasheh of Fetus—Debate Follows Shechitah Dispute

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar's Dictum: Leniency Applies Only to Shechitah, Not to Chelev or Gid
Baraita: Dispute Between Rabbi Meir (Chelev Forbidden) and Rabbi Yehuda (Permitted)
Logic of the Dispute: Considered Limb (No Forbidden Chelev) vs. Own Animal (Chelev Forbidden)
Clarification: The Dispute Is About Chelev Around Gid Hanasheh, Not Fetal Chelev
Reinterpretation: Leniency Applies Only to Eating-Related Issues (Kashrut)

macro_segments.title: Blood of the Fetus: Permitted Even by Those Who Forbid Chelev

micro_segments.title: Resh Lakish: Those Permitting Chelev Permit Blood; Those Forbidding Chelev Forbid Blood
Rabbi Yochanan's Objection: Blood Should Be Forbidden Even If Chelev Permitted
Rav Zeira's Resolution: Blood of Limb Gets Kareis Only When Lifeblood Exists
Application: Fetus Blood Not Subject to Kareis (No Lifeblood in Fetus)

macro_segments.title: Pidyon Bechor: Can Ben Pakuah Be Used to Redeem a Firstborn Donkey?

micro_segments.title: Question: Is Ben Pakuah Considered a Sheep for Redemption Purposes?
According to Rabbi Meir: No Question (Requires Own Shechitah, So It's a Sheep)
According to Rabbanan: Debate—Is It Meat in a Pot or a Living Sheep?
Mar Zutra: Cannot Redeem (Gezeirah Shavah to Korban Pesach)
Rav Sheshet: Can Redeem (Based on Tifdeh Tifdeh to Include Any Sheep)
Rav Ashi's Resolution: Gezeirah Shavah Excludes Ben Pakuah from Pidyon
Conclusion: For Kashrut (Food) Ben Pakuah Is Meat; For Other Purposes It Remains an Animal
